Facts.text: Flavius Anastasius becomes Byzantine Emperor, as Anastasius I - often called the first Byzantine Emperor
Deusdedit II begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Bishop Stanislaus of Kraków is executed by order of Bolesław II of Poland
Battle of Mohi: Mongols led by Batu and Subedei defeat Hungarian King Béla IV in the major battle in during Mongolian invasion of Hungary, with 30,000 Hungarians slain
English premier John Dudley appointed Duke of Northumberland
England and France sign the Treaty of Troyes
Liege Prince-Bishop Robert van Bergen resigns
Venlo joins the Union of Utrecht
Drenthe joins the Union of Utrecht
Last public burning at the stake in England - Edward Wightman for heresy in Lichfield
Anglo-Swedish alliance: commercial trade agreement between England and Sweden is signed
KingWilliam IIIand Queen Mary II crowned as joint rulers of England, Scotland and Ireland
Great Britain, Spain, the Netherlands, Prussia, Savoy, Portugal & France agree the Treaty of Utrecht; France cedes Maritime provinces in North America to Britain
Jack Slack retains the Champion of England boxing title after defeating Frenchman Jean Petit in 7 rounds in Harlston, England, acknowledged as the first international prizefight
Hostilities formally cease in the American Revolutionary War after the Continental Congress proclaims the "Cessation of Arms" against His Britannic Majesty
Johann von Schiller's "Die Jungfrau von Orleans" premieres in Leipzig
Foundation of Bahia Blanca, Argentina
Hungary becomes constitutional monarchy under King Ferdinand of Austria
Steamboat ferry 'Jenny Lind' boiler explosion kills at least 31 passengers in San Francisco Bay, California
Battle of Rivas; Costa Rica beats William Walker's invading Nicaraguans
Confederates surrender at Fort Pulaski, Georgia
Battle of Suffolk, Virginia (Norfleet House)
Battle of Mobile, Alamaba evacuated by Confederates
The Shogunate is abolished in Japan
Spelman College founded as Atlanta Baptist Female Seminary in basement of Friendship Baptist Church, Atlanta
The Concertgebouw in Amsterdam is inaugurated
Ellis Island, New York, designated as an immigration station
8-year old Jewish tailor's daughter disappears in Greece, rumor spreads that she was a Christian girl ritually killed by Jews
Anaheim completes its new electric light system
Hungarian swimmer Alfréd Hajós beats Otto Herschmann of Austria by 0.6s to win the inaugural Olympic 100m freestyle final in 1:22.2 at the Athens Games; also takes out the 1,200m on the same day
Irish tennis player John Boland, representing Great Britain wins both the men's singles and doubles finals at the Athens Olympics; Dionysios Kasdaglis of Greece loses both matches
Treaty of Paris ratifies ends war; Spain cedes Puerto Rico to US
Second Boer War: British forces defeat Boer commandos in Battle of Rooiwal, South Africa
New York Giants future Baseball Hall of Fame backstop Roger Bresnahan becomes first catcher to wear shin guards
Establishment of Tel Aviv by Jewish settlers (named 1910)
Cornerstone of Technion laid in Haifa, Palestine

The UK Parliament introduce a Irish home rule bill, granting Ireland its own bicameral parliament and be required to send a representative to the British House of Commons; Protestants in Ulster resist
The International Labour Organization is founded.
Iowa imposes 1st state cigarette tax
KDKA in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania broadcasts 1st radio sporting event, a boxing match between lightweights Johnny Ray and Johnny Dundee
The Emirate of Transjordan created
Turkestan ASSR forms in Russian SFSR
1st men's college swimming championships begin
Socialists win Danish parliamentary elections
WLS-AM in Chicago IL begins radio transmissions
Abd el-Krims Rifkabylen beats French army in Morocco
Flemish Economic Covenant (VEV) forms in Ghent
Chilean general Carlos Ibáñez names himself president
KLO-AM in Ogden UT begins radio transmissions
Loetafoon celluloid film system demonstrated in Amsterdam
First Butlins holiday camp opens in the UK at Ingoldmells, near Skegness
Hungary leaves League of Nations
Jewish Weekly newspaper taken control by Nazis
Nazi occupiers in Netherlands confiscate Jewish assets
US Merchant Marine Distinguished Service Medal established
American helicopter designer Frank Piasecki, Vertol founder, flies his 1st (single-rotor) craft
RAF bombs census bureau in The Hague
Allied troops liberate Basket-Compascuum
SS burns & shoots 1,100 at Gardelegen
US captures Tsugen Shima
US troops conquers Mulheim, Oberhausen, Bochum, Unna, Essen
US B-29 bomber shot down over Latvia
The Battle of Nanri island takes place.
Oveta Culp Hobby becomes 1st at Health, Education, & Welfare
US Department of Health, Education and Welfare created
French government decides to send 200,000 reservists to Algeria
Singer Nat Cole attacked on stage of Birmingham theater in racial incident
Britain agrees to Singaporean self-rule
Ryan X-13 Vertijet becomes 1st jet to take-off & land vertically
Brooks Hall in Civic Center dedicated in San Francisco, California
Dutch Prince Bernhard visits Lockheed factory
Austrian 4th & last government of Raab resigns
New York Mets make a losing debut
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
40 tornadoes strike US midwest killing 272 & injuring 5,000
Emmett Ashford becomes first African American major league baseball umpire in Washington Senators’ game against the Cleveland Indians at D.C. Stadium
Polish Marshal Spychalski succeeds Ochab as president
Political activist Rudi Dutschke (28) seriously wounded by an East German neo-Nazi's assassination attempt in West Berlin, West Germany
WHED TV channel 15 in Hanover, NH (PBS) begins broadcasting
Apollo 13 launched to Moon; unable to land, returns in 6 days
Beatles' "Let It Be" single goes #1 and stays #1 for 2 weeks
San Francisco beats Cincinnati 2-1, only day Reds aren't in 1st place in 1970
"Johnny Johnson" opens/closes at Edison Theater NYC for 1 performance
WBFF TV channel 45 in Baltimore, MD (IND) begins broadcasting
Benjamin Hooks named to Federal Communications Commission (FCC), 1st African-American to serve
USSR performs underground nuclear test
WW II war criminal JP Philippa arrested
J. P. Parisé scores an 11 sec OT goal, to see the NY Islanders eliminate the NY Rangers in their ever 1st playoff advance
Ireland sets fishing zone at 50 mile
Equal Employment Opportunity Commission regulates sexual harassment
Race riot in London area of Brixton
In the first 'supergrass' trial in Northern Ireland, fourteen Ulster Volunteer Force members are jailed for a total of two hundred years
NASA launches RCA-F
Challenger astronauts complete 1st in space satellite repair
Chinese troops invade Vietnam
Soyuz T-11 returns to Earth
A 1921 Canadian 50 cent coin is auctioned in New York for $22,000
Dodge Morgan sailed solo nonstop around world in 150 days
Halley's Comet makes closest approach to Earth this trip, 63 M km
KXA-AM in Seattle WA changes call letters to KRPM
Yankees score 12 runs in 7th inning vs KC Royals
Zoja Ivanova wins 2nd female World Cup marathon (2:30:39)
Royal Concert building in Amsterdam reopens
Flyers, scores short-handed into an empty net beating Caps 8-5
Customs officers in Middlesbrough, United Kingdom, say they have seized what they believe to be the barrel of a massive gun on a ship bound for Iraq.
MLB California Angels Mark Langston & Mike Witt, no-hit Seattle, 1-0
NY Lotto pays $35 million to two winner (#s are 6-14-24-32-34-51)
NY Rangers beat NY Islanders 6-1, Rangers lead 3-1 in preliminary playoffs
NYC's Museum of Broadcasting becomes "Museum of Radio & Television"
Space Shuttle STS 37 (Atlantis 8) lands at Edwards Air Force Base, California
UN Security Council issues formal cease fire with Iraq declaration
BPAA US Open by Robert Lawrence
Irish Republican Army bombs London financial district, killing 3
MLB Cleveland Indians set team record for long game loss to Boston Red Sox (19 innings - 6½ hr)

450 prisoners riot at the Southern Ohio Correctional Facility in Lucasville, Ohio, and continue for ten days, citing grievances about prison conditions and the forced vaccination of Nation of Islam prisoners (for tuberculosis)
Kirsan Ilumzjinov installed as president of Kalmukkie
Detroit Red Wings become 2nd NHL team to win 60 games in a season
Revival of Rodgers & Hammerstein's musical "The King & I", starring Lou Diamond Phillips and Donna Murphy, premieres at Neil Simon Theater, NYC; runs for 781 performances
MLB: Pacific Bell Park (now Oracle Park) in San Francisco, California and Comerica Park in Detroit, Michigan open
Detained crew of a United States EP-3E aircraft that landed in Hainan, People's Republic of China after a collision with an J-8 fighter are released
The Ghriba synagogue bombing by Al Qaeda kills 21 in Tunisia
Two bombings in the Algerian capital of Algiers kill 33 people and wounds a further 222
"The night of Sora Aoi", Japanese actress and adult video star Sora Aoi joins twitter prompting many Chinese fans to try and circumnavigate Chinese censorship
Minsk Metro bombing in Belarus kills at least 15 and injures over 200
2011 London riot looter is jailed for 11 years after starting a fire at a furniture retailer
8.6 magnitude earthquake and 8.2 aftershock occurs off the coast of Indonesia
Prime Minister of Greece, Lucas Papademos, resigns and calls an election for May 6
South Korean legislative elections result in the governing Saenuri Party retaining governance
14 people are killed in clashes between drug traffickers and police in Michoacán, Mexico
57 people are killed by the Syrian Army in Daraa Governorate, Syria
Fossilized dinosaur eggs with embryos are discovered in China
Two women are beheaded for sorcery in Bougainville, Papua New Guinea
Amy Schumerhosts the 2015 MTV Movie Awards
UN-backed ceasefire comes into effect in Yemen conflict between Iranian-backed Houthis rebels and government forces
Updated "Tree of Life" published in "Nature Microbiology" by scientists from Berkeley University in California
EU leaders agree to a six-month extension to Brexit after UK parliament fails to reach any consensus
First day of voting in Indian general elections, the world's largest democracy with 900 million eligible voters in the world's largest-ever election (till 23rd May)
Israel's Beresheet spacecraft, the first privately-funded mission to the moon, crashes on the moon
Brazil is the 1st country in the southern hemisphere to report more than 1,000 deaths from COVID-19, with 1,056 deaths and 19,638 cases
20-year-old Daunte Wright shot and killed at a traffic stop by police officer in Brooklyn Center, Minnesota, who says they mistook their gun for a taser
Peruvian general election: Free Peru party wins most seats but not a majority, Pedro Castillo wins presidency (not declared till 19 July)
Tropical cyclone Seroja makes landfall in Western Australia near the town of Kalbarri with winds up to 170km/h (105mph)
Mayor of Ukrainian city Mariupol says over 10,000 civilians have died in the Russian siege, with the likely death toll twice this, as bodies are " carpeted through the streets”

Largest cosmic explosion ever recorded (AT2021lwx)- fireball 100 x  size of the solar system with a brightness 2trillion x the sun’s, thought to be large gas cloud entering a supermassive black hole eight million lights years away
Myanmar military kill at least 133 civilians, including children, in an airstrike on Kanbalu township in an area not under control of the military junta
NHL Boston Bruins break record for most team points in a season at 133, in a 5-2 victory over visiting Washington Capitols; Montreal Canadiens held record of 132 since 1977
Russian volcano Shiveluch erupts spewing ash 20 kilometers into the air in the country's eastern Kamchatka region
Bogotá begins water rationing for the first time, as water reservoirs run very low due to drought and El Niño
Vietnamese real estate tycoon Truong My Lan sentenced to death for embezzlement, bribery and banking violations worth 304 trillion dong ($12.46 billion), the country's largest-ever fraud case
China raises US tariffs to 125% in retaliation against Trump excluding China from the 90-day tariff pause and increasing China's tariffs to 145%
Wars of the Roses: KingEdward IVof England seizes London fromHenry VI
Dutch PrinceWilliam I of Orangeflees from Antwerp to Breda
Battle of Cassel: French troops defeat a combined Dutch-Spanish force under William of Orange
Napoléon Bonaparte abdicates unconditionally and is exiled to the island of Elba in the Mediterranean
Robert Schumannhears Italian violin virtuoso Niccolò Paganini play in Frankfurt
Abraham Lincolnurges a spirit of generous conciliation during reconstruction
RMS Titanicleaves Queenstown, Ireland, for NY
Mexican PresidentVictoriano Huertabreaks off diplomatic relations with the US
Hermann Goeringbecomes Premier of Prussia
Stanley Cup Final, Maple Leaf Gardens, Toronto, ON: Detroit Red Wings beat Toronto Maple Leafs, 3-2 for a 3-1 series win; Red Wings first SC Championship
PrinceRainier IIIbecomes ruler of Monaco
US PresidentHarry Trumanrelieves GeneralDouglas MacArthurof command in Korea
"Marty" directed by Delbert Mann and starringErnest Borgnineand Betsy Blair, premieres in New York (Best Picture 1956)
Chartered Air India plane "Kashmir Princess" is bombed and crashes into the South China Sea in a failed assassination attempt onZhou Enlaiby a Kuomintang secret agent
Chilean poetPablo Nerudaarrested in Buenos Aires (later released)
Dodger pitcherDon Drysdalehits his 2nd Opening Day HR
Bob Dylanmakes his 1st appearance at Folk City, Greenwich Village, New York City
Milwaukee Braves future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherWarren Spahnbeats NY Mets, 6-1 for his 328th win; most by a left-hander in MLB history
PopeJohn XXIIIpublishes encyclical "Pacem in terris", that peace between all peoples must be based on truth, justice, love and freedom
Frank Sinatrarecords "Strangers in the Night" single for his album of the same name, later reaches #1 on the Billboard charts
Harlem (NYC) voters defy Congress and re-electAdam Clayton Powell Jr.in special election to fill the seat from which he had been expelled
Tom Stoppard's play "Rosencrantz & Guildenstern are Dead" debuts in London at the Old Vic
US PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonsigns the 1968 Civil Rights Act
Fantasy Records releases "Mardi Gras", the seventh and final studio album by American rock bandCreedence Clearwater Revival
Hank Aaronreturns to County Stadium as a Milwaukee player after his off-season trade from the Atlanta Braves; Brewers beat Cleveland Indians, 6-2
Apple I computer, created bySteve Wozniak, is released
"Luckenbach, Texas (Back to the Basics of Love)" single released byWaylon Jennings(Billboard Song of the Year, 1977)
Tanzanian army captures Kampala, the capital of Uganda forcing Ugandan dictatorIdi Aminto flee into exile in Libya
Paul McCartneyreleases single "Coming Up"
Larry HolmesbeatsTrevor Berbickby unanimous decision in Las Vegas to retain his WBC heavyweight boxing title
Ronald Reaganarrives home from hospital after Hinkley shot him
55th Academy Awards: "Gandhi",Ben Kingsley&Meryl Streepwin
General SecretaryKonstantin Chernenkonamed President of the Soviet Union
60th Academy Awards: "The Last Emperor",Michael Douglas&Cherwin
1st playoff goal scored by a goalie,Ron Hextallof visiting Philadelphia Flyers shoots into Washington Capitols empty net while his team is shorthanded in 8-5 win
An attempted coup d'état takes place in Venezuela against PresidentHugo Chávez.
Iranian PresidentMahmoud Ahmadinejadannounces that Iran has successfully enriched uranium
Michael van Gerwenwins his first tournament victory in the PDC by beating Vincent van der Voort in the final of the Players Championship in Taunton
"TheAvengers", directed byJoss Whedonand starringRobert Downey Jr.,Chris Evans,Chris Hemsworth,Scarlett Johansson,Mark RuffaloandJeremy Renner, premieres in Los Angeles, California
BillionaireWarren Buffettis diagnosed with prostate cancer
Barack ObamaandRaúl Castromeet in Panama, the first meeting of US and Cuban heads of state since the Cuban Revolution
Ex-PopeBenedict XVIclaims Catholic sexual abuse caused in part by 1960s sexual revolution
WikiLeaksfounderJulian Assangeis forcibly removed from the Ecuadorian embassy in London by police and arrested on failure to appear in court on US extradition charges
Shehbaz Sharif elected Pakistan's new Prime Minister by the country's parliament, replacingImran Khan
Battle of Ravenna: French forces under Gaston de Foix defeat the Holy League in a major battle of the Italian Wars
The 12th century Lewis chess pieces are exhibited at the Society of Antiquaries of Scotland, shortly after their rediscovery in a sand bank on the Scottish Isle of Lewis
President McKinley asks for Spanish–American War declaration
The first modern submarine designed and built byJohn Philip Holland, USS Holland is acquired by the U.S. Navy
Germany blitzes Coventry, England
Four soldiers in the Sixth Armored Division of the US Third Army liberate the Nazi concentration camp Buchenwald, freeing thousands includingElie Wiesel
Trial ofAdolf Eichmannfor war crimes in World War II begins in Jerusalem, Israel
Sudanese PresidentOmar al-Bashiris overthrown and arrested by the army in Khartoum after 30 years in power following massive street protests
Andronikos IV Palaiologos, Byzantine Emperor, born in Constantinople, Byzantine Empire (d. 1385)
Frederick I the Warlike, Elector of Saxony, born in Dresden, Margravate of Meissen, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1428)
Roger Mortimer, 4th Earl of March, heir to the throne of England, born in Usk, Monmouthshire (d. 1398)
Marguerite of Navarre, Queen of Navarre, wife of Henry II of Navarre, born in Angoulême, France (d. 1549)
Pietro Della Valle, Italian composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1652)
John Eliot, English parliament leader, born in Port Eliot, Cornwall, England (d. 1632)
Antoine Coypel, French painter and poet, born in Paris (d. 1722)
Anne Danican Philidor, French composer (Concert Spirituel), born in Paris (d. 1728)
Jean-Joseph Mouret, French composer, born in Avignon, France (d. 1738)
John Alcock, English composer and organist, born in London (d. 1806)
David Zeisberger, Moravian clergyman and missionary, born in Zauchtenthal, Moravia (d. 1808)
Christopher Smart, English poet and journalist (Ceremony of Carols), born in Shipbourne, England (d. 1771)
Pierre Nicolas La Houssaye, French violinist and composer, born in Paris (d. 1818)
Wentworth Cheswell, American auditor and teacher, 1st African American elected to public office in the US, born in Newmarket, New Hampshire (d. 1817)
James Parkinson, English physician (First description of Parkinson's disease) and political activist, born in Shoreditch, London (d. 1824)
Johann Georg Lickl, Austrian organist, kapellmeister, and composer, born in Korneuburg, Lower Austria (d. 1843)
Manuel Jose Quintana, Spanish author and poet (El Duque de Viseo), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1857)
Charles-Francois Dumonchau, French pianist and composer, born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1820)
Louise Reichardt, German composer of lieder and scared songs, educator, and choral conductor, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1826)
Nicolaas Christiaan Kist, Dutch Reformed theologian and church historian, born in Woerden, Netherlands (d. 1859)
Edward Everett, American statesman and orator, born in Dorchester, Massachusetts (d. 1865)
Macedonio Melloni, Italian physicist (radiant light), born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (now Italy) (d. 1854)
Claude Tillier, French journalist and writer (My Uncle Benjamin), born in Clamecy, Nièvre, France (d. 1844)
Henry Rawlinson, British army officer and oriental scholar (major role in deciphering cuneiform), born in Chadlington, England (d. 1895)
Clara Fey, German Roman Catholic Nun and the founder of the Sisters of the Poor Child Jesus, born in Aachen, Prussia (d. 1894)
Ferdinand Lassalle, French socialist and political activist (Allgemeiner), born in Breslau, Prussia (d. 1864)
Elmer E. Ellsworth, American soldier who was the 1st Union officer killed in the American Civil War, born in Malta, New York (d. 1861)
Joseph Leopold Röckel, German-British composer, music publisher, and teacher, born in London, England (d. 1908)
John Conrad Nordqvist, Swedish composer, born in Vänersborg, Älvsborg County, Sweden (d. 1920)
Hugh Massie, Australian cricket batsman and captain (9 Tests; NSW), born in Port Fairy, Australia (d. 1938)
Arthur Shrewsbury, English cricket batsman (23 Tests [7 captain]; 1277 runs @ 35 47; Nottinghamshire), born in New Lenton, England (d. 1903)
Constantly Lievens, Flemish missionary in India, born in Moorslede, Belgium (d. 1893)
Basil Harwood, English organist and composer, born in Woodhouse, Gloucestershire, England (d. 1949)
Charles Evans Hughes, American statesman, Republican politician and 11th Chief Justice of Supreme Court (1930-41), born in Glens Falls, New York (d. 1948)
William Wallace Campbell, American astronomer and Director of Lick Observatory, born in Hancock County, Ohio (d. 1938)
Mary White Ovington, American journalist, suffragist, and co-founder of the NAACP, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1951)
Bernard O'Dowd, Australian poet, born in Beaufort, Australia (d. 1953)
Clara Ford, American suffragette supporter and wife of industrialist Henry Ford, born in Wayne County, Michigan (d. 1950)
John Paulus Lotsy, Dutch botanist and geneticist (Resumptio Genetics), born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1931)
Mark Keppel, Superintendent of Los Angeles County Schools (1902-2)8, born in Butte County, California (d. 1928)
Gustav Vigeland, Norwegian sculptor, born in Mandal, Norway (d. 1943)

Edward Lawson, Scottish recipient of the Victoria Cross, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1955)
Paul Henry, Northern Irish artist, born in Belfast, Ireland (d. 1958)

Dean Acheson, American statesman and U.S. Secretary of State (1949-53), born in Middletown, Connecticut (d. 1971)
Johannes Theodoor Thijsse, Dutch director of Delft Hydraulics Laboratory, born in Amsterdam (d. 1984)
Léo-Paul Desrosiers, Quebec novelist (Les Engagés du Grand Portage), born in Berthier, Canada (d. 1967)

Theodor Rogalski, Romanian composer, born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1954)

József Attila, Hungarian poet, born in Ferencváros, Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1937)
Dalia "Dale" Messick, American cartoonist (Brenda Starr, Reporter), born in South Bend, Indiana (d. 2005)
Ivor Spencer-Thomas, English farmer, entrepreneur and inventor, born in Llanymynech, Powys (d. 2001)
Paul Douglas, American actor (Adventure Theater, Clash by Night), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1959)
Jane Bolin, first African American female judge, born in Poughkeepsie, New York (d. 2007)

Henry William Collins, English artist, born in Colchester, Essex (d. 1994)
John Larkin, American actor (Saints & Sinners, The Satan Bug, 12 O'Clock High), born in Oakland, California (d. 1965)

Emilio Colombo, Italian Prime Minister (1970-72), born in Potenza, Basilicata (d. 2013)
Marlen Haushofer, Austrian writer (The Wall), born in Frauenstein, Austria (d. 1970)
Jeff Stollmeyer, West Indian cricket batsman (32 Tests 4 x 100, HS 160; Trinidad & Tobago) and executive (President WI Board of Control 1974-81), born in Santa Cruz, Trinidad (d. 1989)

Alexander Raichev, Bulgarian composer, born in Lom, Bulgaria (d. 2003)
Antoine Blondin, French writer (L'Europe buissonnière), born in Paris (d. 1991)

Gervase de Peyer, British clarinetist, born in London (d. 2017)

Domenico Guaccero, Italian composer, born in Palo de Colle, Italy (d. 1984)

Jack Betts, American character actor (spaghetti Westerns; Gods and Monsters; Spider-Man), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 2025)
Lawrence Coughlin, American lawyer and politician, U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania, born in Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania (d. 2001)
Anton LaVey, American founder of the Church of Satan, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1997)

Clive Exton, British scriptwriter (Poirot; Jeeves and Wooster), and playwright, born in Islington, London (d. 2007)
James Ferman, American director and secretary (British Board of Film Classification), born in New York (d. 2002)
Joseph Burnett-Stuart, British CEO (Robert Fleming Holdings), born in the UK (d. 2010)
Kazuo Fukushima, Japanese composer, born in Tokyo
Nicholas F. Brady, American politician and U.S. Secretary of Treasury (1988-93), born in New York City
Frank Vicari, American jazz, session, and touring saxophonist (Maynard Ferguson; Woody Herman; Tom Waits; Saturday Night Live), born in New Jersey (d. 2006)
Johnny Sheffield, American actor (Boy-Tarzan Finds a Son), born in Pasadena, California (d. 2010)
Koichi Sugiyama, Japanese television and video game composer (Dragon Quest), and Nanjing Massacre denialist, born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 2021)

Lewis Jones, Welsh rugby union outside back (10 Tests Wales, 3 British Lions; Neath, Llanelli) and rugby league utility (2 Tests Wales, 17 Great Britain; Leeds, Wentworthville), born in Gorseinon, Wales (d. 2024)
Francis G. Neubeck, USAF pilot and astronaut, born in Washington, D.C.
Max Schubel, American composer (Opus One Records), born in New York (d. 2010)
Denis Goldberg, South African anti-apartheid activist (convicted alongside Mandela), born in Cape Town (d. 2020)

Tony Brown, American journalist (Tony Brown's Journal), born in Charleston, West Virginia
Anne Poole, British chief nursing officer at the Department of Health (1982-92), birthplace unknown[1]
Mark Strand, American poet, editor and translator (Another Republic), born in Summerside, Prince Edward Island, Canada (d. 2014)
Father Abraham [Pierre Kartner], Dutch singer and songwriter (The Smurf Song), born in Elst, Netherlands (d. 2022)
